salary-not-paid.title: Salary Not Paid Legal Notice
salary-not-paid.h1: Legal Notice for Salary Not Paid in {city}
salary-not-paid.meta_desc: Send a legal notice for unpaid salary in {city}. Recover dues with 10× compensation under Payment of Wages Act. Lawly from ₹199.
salary-not-paid.governing_law: Payment of Wages Act 1936 §15; Industrial Disputes Act 1947; Shops & Establishments Act
salary-not-paid.penalty: Up to 10× the withheld salary as compensation (§15 Payment of Wages Act)
salary-not-paid.time_limit: 12 months from date of wage period where deduction made
what_to_include: Exact months of salary unpaid with amounts
Employment start date and designation
Bank account details where salary was to be credited
Any verbal / written confirmation of dues from employer
what_you_can_claim: Full unpaid salary + arrears
Up to 10× salary as compensation (Payment of Wages Act §15)
Interest at 12% per annum on delayed wages
Retrenchment compensation if you were forced to resign
steps: Send a formal legal notice demanding payment within 15 days
File complaint under Payment of Wages Act with Labour Commissioner in {city}
File at Industrial Tribunal / Labour Court if employer disputes
File simultaneously at DCDRC if services engaged as consumer

faqs.q: Can I send a legal notice for unpaid salary to my employer in {city}?
faqs.a: Yes. A legal notice is the most effective first step. It creates a formal record and often prompts employers to pay immediately to avoid Labour Court proceedings in {city}.

faqs.q: How much compensation can I get for unpaid salary in {city}?
faqs.a: Under Section 15 of the Payment of Wages Act, you can claim up to 10× the withheld amount. The Labour Court in {city} regularly awards this in addition to the unpaid salary.

faqs.q: My employer says the company has no money. Can I still recover salary in {city}?
faqs.a: Yes. The Working Journalists Act and Industrial Disputes Act provide priority claims for workers. Labour Courts in {city} can attach employer property and payroll to recover dues.

property-dispute.title: Property Dispute Legal Notice
property-dispute.h1: Legal Notice for Property Dispute in {city}
property-dispute.meta_desc: Send a legal notice for property encroachment, illegal possession, or landlord-tenant dispute in {city}. Lawly from ₹199.
property-dispute.governing_law: Transfer of Property Act 1882; Specific Relief Act 1963 §6 §38; Registration Act 1908
property-dispute.governing_law_short: Specific Relief Act 1963 §6 + Transfer of Property Act
property-dispute.forum: Civil Court / City Civil Court / District Court in {city}
property-dispute.penalty: Mandatory injunction to vacate + damages + criminal trespass (IPC §441)
what_to_include: Full property description (survey number, area, address)
Title / ownership documents you hold
Specific acts of encroachment or illegal possession
Demand to vacate / stop interference within 30 days
what_you_can_claim: Recovery of possession (Specific Relief Act §6)
Permanent injunction against future interference
Mesne profits (rental value of property during illegal occupation)
Damages for any destruction or alteration to property
steps: Send a formal legal notice demanding vacation/restoration within 30 days
File suit under Specific Relief Act §6 in City Civil Court {city} (no title proof needed within 6 months)
Apply for interim injunction to prevent further encroachment
File criminal complaint for trespass under BNS §329 (old IPC §441)

faqs.q: How quickly can I recover my property in {city} after sending a legal notice?
faqs.a: Under Section 6 of the Specific Relief Act, you can file for recovery of possession within 6 months without proving title. City Civil Court {city} issues interim injunctions within days.

faqs.q: My tenants won't vacate in {city}. Do I need a legal notice first?
faqs.a: Yes. A legal notice is mandatory before filing an eviction suit. Send a notice terminating tenancy citing non-payment or expiry of term, then file in Rent Court or Civil Court {city}.

faqs.q: My neighbour is encroaching on my property boundary. What can I do in {city}?
faqs.a: Send a legal notice with survey map attached. File a civil suit for mandatory injunction in City Civil Court {city}. Also file a police complaint for criminal trespass (BNS §329).

defamation.title: Defamation Legal Notice
defamation.meta_desc: Send a legal notice for defamation, false accusations, or online harassment in {city}. Claim damages. Lawly from ₹199.
defamation.governing_law: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 §356; IT Act 2000 §66A (civil); Civil Defamation under Tort Law
defamation.penalty: Up to 2 years imprisonment (BNS §356) + unlimited civil damages
defamation.time_limit: 1 year for criminal complaint; 6 years for civil suit (Limitation Act)
what_to_include: Exact defamatory statement(s) with date, time, platform
Evidence: screenshots, links, witness names, chat logs
How the statement is false and damaged your reputation
Demand for public apology + takedown + damages
what_you_can_claim: Financial damages for reputation loss
Mandatory apology (civil suit specific performance)
Content takedown under IT Rules 2021 (social media platforms)
Criminal prosecution under BNS §356 (imprisonment up to 2 years)
steps: Preserve all evidence: screenshots, timestamps, witness names
Send a legal notice demanding apology, takedown, and damages
Report to social media platform citing IT Rules 2021 (takedown in 36 hours for criminal content)
File civil suit for damages OR criminal complaint under BNS §356 in {city}

faqs.q: Can I sue someone for defamatory social media posts in {city}?
faqs.a: Yes. Both civil and criminal defamation actions lie under Indian law. The Sessions Court in {city} has jurisdiction for criminal defamation under BNS §356 (up to 2-year imprisonment).

faqs.q: My ex-employer gave a false reference. Can I send a defamation notice?
faqs.a: Yes. False statements to prospective employers causing job loss are actionable as defamation. A Lawly legal notice to the employer creates a formal record and often ends the behaviour.

faqs.q: Does sending a legal notice get defamatory content taken down quickly?
faqs.a: Yes. Under IT Rules 2021, social media platforms must remove content notified as criminal defamation within 36 hours. A legal notice to the platform triggers this obligation.

medical-negligence.title: Medical Negligence Legal Notice
medical-negligence.h1: Legal Notice for Medical Negligence in {city}
medical-negligence.meta_desc: Send a legal notice to a hospital or doctor for medical negligence in {city}. Claim compensation. Lawly from ₹199.
medical-negligence.governing_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019; IMA v. VP Shantha (1995 SC); NMC Act 2020; Tort law
medical-negligence.governing_law_short: CPA 2019 + IMA v. VP Shantha (1995 SC)
medical-negligence.forum: DCDRC {city} / High Court / National Consumer Commission
medical-negligence.penalty: Compensation from ₹50,000 to ₹5 crore+ depending on harm
medical-negligence.time_limit: 2 years from date of negligence or discovery
what_to_include: Full treatment timeline with dates
Medical records and discharge summary
Expert opinion documenting the negligence
Exact financial loss and health harm caused
what_you_can_claim: All treatment costs for corrective procedures
Income loss during recovery period
Compensation for permanent disability or disfigurement
Compensation for death of family member (loss of dependency)
steps: Obtain all medical records (hospital must provide within 72 hours)
Get independent medical expert opinion on the negligence
Send a legal notice to the hospital's Medical Superintendent
File at DCDRC {city} or High Court for larger claims

faqs.q: Can I send a legal notice to a private hospital in {city} for negligence?
faqs.a: Yes. Since the Supreme Court's ruling in IMA v. VP Shantha (1995), medical services are covered under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. DCDRC {city} handles all medical negligence complaints.

faqs.q: How much compensation can I get for medical negligence in {city}?
faqs.a: Courts in {city} have awarded ₹50 lakh to ₹5 crore+ for cases involving death, permanent disability, or gross negligence. The amount depends on income loss, age, and severity of harm.

faqs.q: The hospital won't give me medical records. What can I do in {city}?
faqs.a: Send a Lawly legal notice (₹199) citing the Hospital's legal obligation under MCI regulations and the RTI Act (if government hospital). Non-compliance can be cited as evidence of concealment in court.

cheque-bounce.title: Cheque Bounce Legal Notice
cheque-bounce.h1: Legal Notice for Cheque Bounce in {city}
cheque-bounce.meta_desc: Send a statutory legal notice for cheque bounce to recover your money in {city}. NI Act §138. Lawly from ₹199.
cheque-bounce.forum: Magistrate Court / Metropolitan Magistrate Court in {city}
cheque-bounce.notice_period: 30 days (mandatory — must be sent within 30 days of dishonour)
cheque-bounce.penalty: Up to 2 years imprisonment + twice the cheque amount
cheque-bounce.time_limit: 30 days from dishonour to send notice; then 15 days for payment; then file within 30 days
what_to_include: Cheque number, date, amount, drawee bank
Date of dishonour and bank return memo reason
Nature of underlying debt / liability
Demand for payment within 15 days of receiving this notice
what_you_can_claim: Full cheque amount + 12% interest
Up to 2× cheque amount as additional fine
Legal costs and expenses
Criminal prosecution leading to imprisonment of drawer
steps: Get the cheque return memo from your bank immediately
Send legal notice within 30 days of dishonour (mandatory — or you lose the case)
If no payment in 15 days, file complaint under §138 NI Act in Magistrate Court {city}
Court issues summons; upon conviction: up to 2 years jail + fine of 2× cheque amount

faqs.q: How urgent is it to send a cheque bounce legal notice in {city}?
faqs.a: Very urgent. Under NI Act §138, the legal notice MUST be sent within 30 days of cheque dishonour. Missing this deadline permanently bars you from filing a criminal complaint in {city}.

faqs.q: What if the person pays after receiving the notice?
faqs.a: That's a successful outcome. Most cheque bounce matters settle within the 15-day notice period. If they pay, you do not need to file in court.

faqs.q: Can I file a cheque bounce case at a court in {city} against someone in another city?
faqs.a: Yes. Under the Supreme Court's ruling in Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod (2015), cheque bounce complaints are filed in the city where the cheque was presented for payment (i.e., your bank in {city}).

rent-recovery.title: Rent Recovery Legal Notice
rent-recovery.h1: Legal Notice for Rent Recovery in {city}
rent-recovery.meta_desc: Send a legal notice to your tenant for unpaid rent in {city}. Evict and recover dues quickly. Lawly from ₹199.
rent-recovery.governing_law: Transfer of Property Act 1882; Rent Control Act applicable to {city}; Code of Civil Procedure 1908
rent-recovery.governing_law_short: Transfer of Property Act + State Rent Control Act
rent-recovery.forum: Rent Control Court / City Civil Court in {city}
rent-recovery.penalty: Eviction decree + all rent arrears + mesne profits + legal costs
rent-recovery.time_limit: 3 years from date of each rent default (Limitation Act)
what_to_include: Lease agreement details (date, term, monthly rent)
Exact arrears with month-wise breakup
Prior reminders (verbal or written)
Demand for payment within 15 days or vacate premises
what_you_can_claim: All rent arrears with interest
Mesne profits (double rent) for period after tenancy termination
Cost of any damage to property
Legal costs of eviction proceedings
steps: Send legal notice terminating tenancy and demanding arrears
If no response, file eviction suit in Rent Court / Civil Court {city}
Apply for summary judgment / ex-parte decree if tenant absent
Execute decree: attach tenant's assets or get court bailiff to supervise exit

faqs.q: Can I evict my tenant in {city} without going to court?
faqs.a: No. Self-help eviction (cutting power, changing locks, removing belongings) is illegal. A legal notice followed by a court eviction suit is the proper path. Courts in {city} issue eviction decrees within 3–6 months for clear-cut defaults.

faqs.q: My tenant hasn't paid rent for 3 months in {city}. What can I do?
faqs.a: Send a Lawly legal notice (₹199) terminating the tenancy and claiming arrears. Many tenants pay upon receiving a legal notice to avoid eviction. If not, file in Rent Court {city} immediately.

faqs.q: Does rent control protection apply even if my tenant doesn't pay?
faqs.a: Non-payment of rent is a ground for eviction even under strict rent control laws. {city}'s Rent Court regularly grants eviction for repeated defaults. A legal notice is the mandatory first step.

consumer-complaint.title: Consumer Complaint Legal Notice
consumer-complaint.h1: Legal Notice for Consumer Complaint in {city}
consumer-complaint.meta_desc: Send a legal notice before filing your consumer complaint in {city}. Forces companies to settle. Lawly from ₹199.
consumer-complaint.governing_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019; Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020
consumer-complaint.forum: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (DCDRC) in {city}
consumer-complaint.penalty: Compensation + punitive damages + product recall under CPA 2019
consumer-complaint.time_limit: 2 years from date of deficiency of service or unfair trade practice
what_to_include: Invoice / receipt / order confirmation
Exact deficiency: what was promised vs. what was delivered
Prior complaints made to company and responses
Amount of compensation demanded
what_you_can_claim: Full refund or replacement
Compensation for mental agony and inconvenience
Legal costs
Punitive damages for wilful disregard under CPA 2019 §39
steps: Send a legal notice to the company's Grievance Officer
File complaint on National Consumer Helpline (consumerhelpline.gov.in / 1915)
If unresolved in 30 days, file at DCDRC in {city}
DCDRC is free for claims up to ₹50 lakh; no lawyer needed

faqs.q: Do I need to send a legal notice before filing at DCDRC in {city}?
faqs.a: No — it's not legally required. But a pre-litigation legal notice often settles the matter in 15 days, saving you months of court hearings. It's the smartest first step.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to file a consumer complaint in {city}?
faqs.a: Filing fees at DCDRC {city} are: ₹50 (claims up to ₹5L), ₹200 (claims up to ₹10L), ₹400 (up to ₹20L), ₹500 (up to ₹50L). No lawyer needed. A Lawly legal notice (₹199) is the fastest first step.

faqs.q: How long does a consumer case take at DCDRC in {city}?
faqs.a: CPA 2019 mandates resolution within 90 days. In practice, {city}'s DCDRC resolves most cases within 3–6 months. Sending a legal notice first can settle things in 15–30 days without any court visit.

employment-termination.title: Wrongful Termination Legal Notice
employment-termination.h1: Legal Notice for Wrongful Termination in {city}
employment-termination.meta_desc: Send a legal notice for wrongful termination or illegal dismissal in {city}. Recover dues and compensation. Lawly from ₹199.
employment-termination.governing_law: Industrial Disputes Act 1947; Shops & Establishments Act; Payment of Gratuity Act 1972
employment-termination.governing_law_short: Industrial Disputes Act 1947 + Shops & Establishments Act
employment-termination.penalty: Reinstatement OR full back wages + gratuity + retrenchment compensation
employment-termination.time_limit: 3 years for Labour Court; 1 year recommended for best outcome
what_to_include: Employment contract / appointment letter details
Date of termination and manner of termination
Notice period terms in contract and whether followed
Any prior disciplinary actions claimed by employer
what_you_can_claim: Notice pay (salary in lieu of notice)
Retrenchment compensation (15 days per year served for workmen)
Full gratuity (if 5+ years served, Payment of Gratuity Act)
Back wages for the period from termination to reinstatement
steps: Send a legal notice disputing the termination and claiming dues
File complaint under Industrial Disputes Act before Labour Commissioner {city}
If conciliation fails, the matter goes to Labour Court / Industrial Tribunal {city}
For senior executives (non-workmen): file civil suit for breach of contract

faqs.q: Can I fight a wrongful termination in {city} even without a written contract?
faqs.a: Yes. Offer letter, salary slips, and appointment order are sufficient evidence. Industrial Courts in {city} regularly reinstate workers terminated without proper procedure.

faqs.q: My employer fired me via WhatsApp. Is that valid?
faqs.a: Probably not. Under the Industrial Disputes Act, termination requires written notice (usually 30–90 days) and proper dues payment. A verbal or WhatsApp termination without dues is challengeable in Labour Court {city}.

faqs.q: What if I was a senior manager, not a 'workman'?
faqs.a: Senior managers not covered under the Industrial Disputes Act can file a civil suit for breach of contract in City Civil Court {city} and claim notice pay, unpaid bonuses, and damages.
